title: Listening as a State the Body Enters

paragraphs: In the Arab musical tradition, the highest praise that can be given to a performer is not that they played beautifully but that they made the audience reach a particular state. The state has a name that does not translate cleanly into European musical vocabulary. The musician's task, on this account, is not to produce sound but to produce in the listener a condition the listener cannot enter alone — a sustained, embodied, almost physiological transport in which the boundary between hearing and being heard begins to dissolve.

Tarab is the Arabic word for this. It names both the state itself and the music capable of inducing it. The word is at least a thousand years old, attested in the writings of the medieval Arab music theorists who treated the production of tarab as the central problem of musical aesthetics. The classical Arab modes — the maqamat, of which there are dozens — were not understood as decorative scales but as specific emotional and somatic territories, each capable of producing a different quality of tarab if the performer was sufficiently skilled and the audience sufficiently present. The musician's relationship to the audience was not performance but collaborative arrival.

What is striking about the institution of tarab, from the perspective of a European concert-hall tradition, is how much active work the audience is expected to do. Recordings of Umm Kulthum, the Egyptian singer who dominated Arab music for half of the twentieth century, document audiences who shout, weep, repeat phrases back, request the same line again so that she would sing it a second or third or fifth time, each time with subtle variation, until the room had reached together the state none of them could have reached alone. The European concert, by contrast, asks its audience to remain silent and immobile and to applaud only at sanctioned moments. The two traditions are not differently polite. They are working on different theories of what music is for.

The deeper claim implicit in the institution of tarab is that listening is not a passive reception of sound but an active state the body enters. This is not metaphorical. The autonomic responses recorded in audiences during a sustained tarab performance — heart rate, skin conductance, pupil dilation — are measurably different from the responses recorded in audiences attending the same singers in studio playback. Something happens in the room that does not happen on the record. The presence of the audience changes the singer's choices, and the singer's responsiveness changes the audience's state, and the recording captures the sound while leaving the actual event behind.

This has implications for what we have lost in the recording era. The dominant form of musical experience for most people most of the time has become solitary listening to fixed playback through small loudspeakers held close to the ear. The architecture of this experience is structurally incompatible with the production of tarab, because tarab requires sustained collective presence with a performer who is responding in real time to the room. What is available through earbuds is not a degraded version of tarab. It is a different category of activity, with its own pleasures, but the loss of the older category is real and is not compensated for by the gain.

What resists in this is the assumption that more access to music is straightforwardly more music. The recording industry has put more sound into more ears than at any previous moment in human history, and has done so largely at the expense of the social and architectural conditions under which the oldest and most demanding form of musical experience was ever possible. The tarab tradition has not disappeared — it continues in private gatherings in Cairo and Beirut and Damascus, in the long evenings during which the music takes the time it needs to take — but it has become a minority practice in a global musical culture that has substantially forgotten what it was the older form was doing.
essay.blockquote: The recording captures the sound while leaving the actual event behind — and the event was the music.

positions: The Arab musical tradition has carried for a thousand years an understanding of musical experience that the European concert and the recording era have collectively allowed to atrophy. What tarab names — the sustained collective state produced by live music in a responsive audience — is not nostalgia or mysticism but a specific, demonstrable, partially measurable phenomenon, and the contemporary global musical culture has been built around technologies that systematically preclude it.

The romanticisation of tarab risks treating one cultural form as the universal standard against which other forms are deficient, when in fact different musical traditions are working on different problems and the comparison between them is not an evaluation. The earbud listener walking through a city to a recorded album is having an experience as old as the city itself; it is not a substitute for tarab any more than tarab is a substitute for it. The two coexist and neither is the truth of the other.

number.label: Approximate number of times Umm Kulthum reportedly sang a single phrase from her song 'Inta Omri' during a 1964 concert in Cairo, varying the inflection on each repetition in response to the audience, in pursuit of the moment of collective tarab that the song had not yet reached.

fragment: The room had been holding the same chord for what felt like hours. The musician played one phrase, paused, played it slightly differently, paused, played the third variation, and on the third variation the older woman in the corner began to cry without sound and the boy on her left, who had been bored ten minutes earlier, took her hand and did not understand why he had done so. The musician closed his eyes and continued. He had been listening for the room to do exactly this. He had played the same song for thirty years waiting to find out what it could do.

word.origin: Arabic; from a triliteral root meaning agitation, excitation, emotional movement. The term is attested in classical Arab music theory at least since the ninth-century treatises of al-Kindi, and remains central to the theoretical and practical vocabulary of Arab music today. It is used as both a noun (the state itself) and an adjective (music capable of producing the state), and is the standard term in modern Arabic for what European traditions describe variously as ecstasy, rapture, or transport — though none of those terms quite captures the bidirectional, collaborative quality the Arabic word carries.
word.definition: The state of musical ecstasy produced in a listener by a sufficiently skilled performance of the right music in the right conditions — a sustained, embodied, almost physiological transport in which the listener becomes part of the music's production rather than its passive recipient. Also used to name the music capable of inducing the state, and the audience's audible participation in its arrival. Distinct from European concepts of musical pleasure in its insistence on the active, collaborative, and somatic character of the experience.

quote.text: إن الموسيقى مرآة الروح، ولكنها مرآة لا تنعكس إلا أمام من يصغي بقلبه.
quote.translation: Music is a mirror of the soul, but a mirror that does not reflect except before the one who listens with the heart.
quote.author: Sayyid Darwish
quote.source: Attributed in interviews collected in 'Sayyid Darwish: Hayatuhu wa-A'maluhu' (Sayyid Darwish: His Life and Works), 1923

on_this_day.text: On 6 June 1944, the largest amphibious assault in military history put approximately 156,000 Allied troops onto the beaches of Normandy in a single day. The operation was code-named Overlord and the day itself, in subsequent military usage, became simply 'D-Day.' The figures admit of various interpretations; the most consequential one, for the purposes of this digest, is the silence that fell across the BBC Home Service in the days following, as the announcers read out the lists of confirmed dead — a form of collective listening, conducted in shared real time across a country, that has almost no equivalent in the post-broadcast media environment, and whose absence as a national experience the contemporary world is still in the process of learning to register.

person.name: A. J. Racy
person.dates: 1943 – present · Beirut, Lebanon / Los Angeles, USA
paragraphs: Ali Jihad Racy is the Lebanese-American ethnomusicologist whose 2003 book 'Making Music in the Arab World: The Culture and Artistry of Ṭarab' is the standard English-language scholarly account of the tradition. Racy was trained both as a performer — he is among the most distinguished contemporary players of the buzuq, a long-necked fretted lute used in Eastern Mediterranean music — and as a scholar, with a doctorate from the University of Illinois and a long career at UCLA, where he taught Arab music and ethnomusicology for more than four decades. The book combined his insider's understanding of the musical practice with the analytical apparatus of academic ethnomusicology in a way that had not previously been done for this material in English.

Racy's complication is that the audience for the tradition he documents has continued to contract during precisely the decades of his teaching, partly through the dispersal of the urban Levantine concert culture that nurtured it (the long wars in Lebanon, Syria, and Iraq have done substantial damage to the social institutions tarab required), and partly through the global standardisation of musical experience around the formats of the recording industry. He has spent his career carrying forward, in both performance and scholarship, a tradition whose practical conditions of possibility have been progressively narrowing — a position familiar to many of the most distinguished ethnomusicologists of his generation but one that has its own particular weight when the music in question is from one's native culture.

now_here.name: Beit Sitti's Riad Courtyard
now_here.text: A nineteenth-century Damascene courtyard house in the old city near the Umayyad Mosque, of the kind that across the older quarters of the Levant has historically served as the architectural setting for the sahra — the long evening of music, food, and conversation in which tarab was traditionally produced. The courtyard is open to the sky, with a small fountain at its centre and inward-facing rooms arranged around it; the acoustics of such a space, with the surrounding plaster walls and the open ceiling, are unlike any built specifically for music in the European tradition. Many of these houses across Syria have been damaged or destroyed in the years since 2011; the surviving ones, in Damascus and in the Beirut quarters that retain something of the same architecture, hold the physical memory of the conditions under which the music was made.

questions.text: When was the last time you listened to music with the full physical attention the tarab tradition assumes — without doing anything else, in a room organised around the listening rather than around something else?
questions.deeper: What is it that the way you usually listen to music — in the background, while walking, while working — has come to substitute for? And do you experience the substitution as a loss or as a different and equally valid form of musical life?
questions.experiment: Choose one piece of music you know well and listen to it once this week with no other activity, no phone, no movement, sitting in a single chair. Notice what arrives in your attention that has not arrived during your usual listening.

questions.text: Have you ever been present in an audience that reached together a state none of its members could have reached alone — a concert, a service, a reading, a meeting in which the room itself became the medium?
questions.deeper: What were the conditions that allowed it to happen, and to what extent were those conditions architectural, social, or accidental? And does the experience remain a benchmark for what gathered attention can produce, or has it faded into a remembered exception?
questions.experiment: If a chance to be in such a room presents itself in the coming weeks — live music, ceremony, a shared performance — go, even if the practical conditions of going are inconvenient. Notice afterwards what the room produced that the recording will not.

questions.text: The Arab musical tradition treats listening as an active state the body enters. Is there a domain of your life — reading, conversation, observation — in which you would say the same about your attention, and one in which you would not?
questions.deeper: Could the more active form of attention from one domain be transferred to another, or are the modes specific to their materials? And what would it cost you to attempt the transfer?
questions.experiment: Identify one domain in which your attention is habitually distributed and one in which it is habitually concentrated. Attempt, for one day, to swap them: bring concentrated attention to the distributed domain, and notice both what becomes available and what becomes difficult.

trivia.question: Which Egyptian singer, whose monthly Thursday-night radio broadcasts from Cairo halted public life across much of the Arab world for more than three decades, is most closely associated with the modern practice of tarab?
trivia.explanation: Umm Kulthum (c. 1898–1975) dominated Arab music for the central decades of the twentieth century, and her concerts — broadcast monthly on the first Thursday of the month from the Cairo Opera House between 1937 and 1973 — became one of the few media events of the period to attract audiences of more than 100 million across the Arab-speaking world. Her live performances, in which she would repeat phrases dozens of times in response to audience reaction, are the most fully documented case of sustained collective tarab in the modern record. Fairuz, Asmahan, and Warda were all major figures, but Umm Kulthum's particular institutional role in the tradition has no twentieth-century equal.

word_a.meaning: musical ecstasy; a state of embodied transport produced collaboratively by performer and audience

word_b.meaning: to find, to compose poetry; the root of the troubadour tradition of medieval Provence

data.question: These two words sound similar and both belong to the history of musical performance. Are they true cognates with a shared etymological root, or false cognates that resemble each other by coincidence?
data.explanation: Despite the phonetic similarity and the shared semantic field of music and poetry, the words have no etymological relationship. 'Ṭarab' derives from a Semitic root denoting emotional agitation. 'Trobar' derives from a Late Latin verb meaning to find or invent (related to the modern French 'trouver'). The folk etymology that derives 'troubadour' from 'ṭarab' has been proposed since the nineteenth century and is occasionally revived, but the historical-linguistic evidence does not support it.

movie.title: The Time That Remains
movie.text: The third film in Suleiman's loose autobiographical trilogy, tracing his Palestinian-Israeli family from 1948 to the present in a series of static, painterly compositions interrupted by sudden absurd gestures. The film's relationship to listening is structural: long sequences hold on rooms in which the radio is playing — Umm Kulthum, news bulletins, the BBC Arabic Service — while the family conducts itself silently around the broadcast. The radio is not background. It is a character. The film documents what it means, across three generations, to live with one's primary public soundtrack arriving from a state of affairs one has not consented to and cannot turn off.
